title: Shakespeare Ipsum
text:
Hark! The dawn awakens with golden breath upon the weary world, and gentle zephyrs whisper secrets to the morn. Methinks the stars do conspire in silence, weaving fates with threads of silver ‘cross the boundless sky.

 Love is not love which alters when it alteration finds. Oh no, it is an ever fixed mark that looks on tempests and is never shaken. It is the star to every wandering bark whose worth's unknown tho his height be taken.

Lo, the heart's ambition doth outpace the feet of reason, and thus we stumble toward our dreams.

A man may wear a crown of gold, yet still his soul may hunger for a simpler peace.

By my troth, fortune's wheel doth spin as wildly as a drunkard's tale at tavern's close.

Fair love, thou art both poison and balm, sweet torment of the mortal breast.

The night doth cloak all sins in velvet shadow, yet truth shall find its light ere long.

Soft! What murmur through the gardens breaks? ‘Tis but the breath of roses sighing in their sleep.

The fool oft speaketh wisdom clothed in jest, while sages hide their folly in fine words.

Mark well the time, for hours flee faster than falcons through a storm.

A jest too long is like a feast o'er-stayed — delight turns weary in the telling.

Thy tongue is sharp as any rapier, and twice as quick to draw.

Though kingdoms fall and empires fade, the poet's quill shall outlast them all.

Sweet melancholy, thou companion fair, thou mak'st of sorrow a noble art.

The wind doth sigh upon the heath as if in mourning for forgotten kings.

A heart untested knoweth not its strength; a sword unblooded knoweth not its edge.

To be content is to conquer the self, though no banners fly for such a victory.

The moon doth watch the lovers' tryst and blush behind a cloud.

In faith, the world's a stage, and we the actors strut till time pulls down the curtain.

A promise broken is a mirror cracked — it showeth truth, but ever flawed.

The wine of youth is sweet, yet bitter grows when age remembers it.

Fair words may gild deceit as gold adorns a lie.

The sea doth roar with tempests wild, but calmer hearts may steer through any storm.

Dreams are but messengers from realms unseen, whispering truths the waking mind forgets.

The raven croaks upon the battlements — a herald of ill or omen of change.

A beggar with courage is richer than a king who fears his crown.

He who flatters holds a knife unseen, sheathed ‘neath a smile.

There is music in all things if thou hast ears to hear it.

The flame of passion doth consume as swiftly as it warms.

Wisdom oft comes late, trailing shame behind her like a shadow.

Methinks the hour grows old, and yet our hearts grow young with foolish hope.

Beauty is a fleeting guest — cherish her ere she departs.

The rose hath thorns, and so doth love, yet still we reach to pluck it.

To speak plain truth in courts of lies is peril wrapped in honor.

O, sweet sorrow, thou faithful friend who never leav'st the soul untried.

The quill doth pierce more hearts than swords, and leaves no blood to show.

No tempest can unmake the mountain's will, nor doubt the steadfast heart.

What fools are men, to chase the wind and call it destiny!

Thou art as constant as the northern star, though fates may twist and time may fade.

Gold may buy a crown, but never sleep without regret.

When silence speaks, the wise would do well to listen.

The sun itself must bow to night, yet rises proud each morn anew.

Kindness costs but little coin, yet spend it well and thou art rich indeed.

The eyes are windows where the soul doth lean to look upon the world.

Love's labor, once lost, is never wholly gone — it lingers like a ghost in memory's hall.

The jesters of the court oft see the truest kings.

If honor be thy guide, then fear no tempest nor tyrant's wrath.

O gentle heart, why beat so fast for things that cannot stay?

The storm that bends the tree but strengthens its root.

A fool's courage may win where wisdom trembles.

Words once loosed take wing and never may return.

Each man doth bear two masks — one for the world, and one for his own heart.

In every jest there hides a shard of truth, sharp enough to wound.

The stars above care not for mortal sorrow, yet they shine to guide us still.

No feast is sweet to one who eats alone.

When envy wakes, friendship sleeps.

The quiver of time hath arrows enough for all men — none know when their turn shall come.

The fairest face may hide the foulest plot.

Let not pride be thy captain, lest it steer thee to the rocks.

Faith untested is but fancy clothed in virtue's guise.

A heart that loves too well forgets the cost until it bleeds.

Patience is a gentle queen who conquers all by waiting.

When kings do quarrel, the earth itself doth tremble.

The smallest truth outweighs a mountain of deceit.

O fleeting joy! Thou com'st on swan's wings and depart'st on raven's.

A jest may heal where sermons fail. Love's not time's fool tho rosy lips and cheeks within his bending sickles compass comes. Love alters not with his brief hours and weeks but bears it out even to the edge of doom.

The candle of hope doth burn even in the darkest vault.

To thine own heart be true, though the world call thee mad.

The grave makes equals of us all, from beggar's sack to monarch's robe.

The fairest flower blooms where none dare tread.

There is no silence like that which follows parting words.

A gentle soul may tame a beast more surely than a whip.

In wine and love, the truth doth spill unbidden.

No oath so strong that time cannot unbind it.

The heavens weep for men who waste their days on trifles.

Where fortune smiles, envy waits close behind.

Thou canst not pluck a star, yet still men try.

The sweetest songs are born from hearts that ache.

The seed of mercy groweth best in humble soil.

To conquer another is but triumph brief; to master thyself is glory everlasting.

When folly dances, wisdom waits her turn.

The ghost of guilt doth haunt the sleepless mind.

A friend in mirth is easy found; in sorrow, rare as angels' tears.

Fair youth must fade, but virtue blooms eternal.

The world's applause is but the echo of vanity.

Speak softly when thy words bear weight, lest they crush whom they should guide.

Fortune favors not the bold, but the cunning who feign boldness.

The mind's tempest wrecks more ships than any storm.

Where greed takes root, all else withers.

He who mocks love shall someday kneel to it.

The hours slip silent as a thief, stealing youth and leaving wisdom's scars.

Even shadows flee from light too bright to bear.

The crown of peace rests easiest upon the humble brow.

In truth, the heart doth know before the mind admits.

The gods may jest with mortal fates, yet man must play his part with grace.

A wound of words may fester longer than one of steel.

Thus ends the song, yet still the echo lingers on the air, a whisper of what was and what shall never be again.
